topics.title: Wrapper Classes & Autoboxing
topics.description: Every primitive in Java (int, long, double, boolean…) has a corresponding Wrapper class (Integer, Long, Double, Boolean…) in java.lang. Wrappers let primitives participate in collections, generics, and reflection. Autoboxing is the compiler silently converting int ↔ Integer; unboxing does the reverse. The Integer cache (-128 to 127) is a classic interview trap.
topics.story: Imagine a VIP club (Collections API) that only lets in people with name badges (objects). Raw int values are anonymous — no badge, no entry. Wrapper classes are the badge-printing station: they wrap the raw value in an object so it can enter the club. Autoboxing is the bouncer doing the wrapping automatically so you don't have to think about it. But watch out — two badges printed for the same VIP number above 127 are different physical objects (== fails).
topics.analogy: Primitive int = cash (lightweight, no identity). Integer = a cheque (heavyweight object with identity). Autoboxing = bank prints a cheque for your cash automatically. Cache = bank keeps pre-printed cheques for small amounts (-128–127) — same cheque object reused. == compares cheque identity; equals() compares the written amount.
topics.memoryAnchor: Primitives: byte,short,int,long,float,double,char,boolean. Wrappers: Byte,Short,Integer,Long,Float,Double,Character,Boolean. Cache: Integer.valueOf(-128..127) same ref. Autobox: int→Integer (boxing), Integer→int (unboxing). Unboxing null → NullPointerException. parseInt/valueOf difference: parseInt returns int, valueOf returns Integer.
topics.firstPrinciples: Java generics and collections are erased to Object at runtime — they cannot hold primitives. Wrapper classes bridge the gap. The Integer cache exists because small integers are so common that reusing instances saves heap allocation. Autoboxing is purely syntactic sugar inserted by javac.
topics.bruteForce: Pre-autoboxing (Java 1.4): List list = new ArrayList(); list.add(Integer.valueOf(42)); int val = ((Integer)list.get(0)).intValue(); — manual boxing/unboxing everywhere. Verbose, error-prone, easy to forget intValue().
topics.optimizedApproach: Java 5+: List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(42); int val = list.get(0); — compiler inserts boxing/unboxing. But beware: repeated autoboxing in loops (for(int i=0;i<n;i++) sum+=list.get(i)) creates n Integer objects. Use intStream or int[] when performance matters.
topics.whenToUse: Use Integer/Long/Double when: storing in collections, using as generic type params, needing null to represent 'absent', calling static utility methods (Integer.parseInt, Integer.MAX_VALUE, Integer.toBinaryString). Use primitives for local variables, arrays, and performance-critical loops to avoid boxing overhead and GC pressure.

examples.title: Autoboxing, Cache Trap & Unboxing NPE
examples.explanation: The cache trap (== for Integer -128..127) is one of the most common Java interview questions. Autoboxing hides heap allocation — in tight loops it causes GC pressure. Always use equals() for Integer comparison. Unboxing a null wrapper throws NPE — guard with null checks.
examples.realWorldUse: HashMap<Integer,Integer> autoboxes keys/values. Optional<Integer> vs OptionalInt. Spring @Value injection into Integer fields. JSON deserialization into Integer allows null. JdbcTemplate returns Integer for nullable DB columns.

examples.title: Enums — Constants, Fields, Methods, Switch
examples.explanation: Enums are compile-safe constants — no magic strings or ints. They can carry fields and methods, making them behaviour-rich. Enum constructors are private. switch on enum is exhaustive with pattern matching. name() returns string, ordinal() returns position (don't persist ordinal — it breaks on reorder). Use EnumSet/EnumMap for enum-keyed collections — faster than HashMap.
examples.realWorldUse: Status enums (OrderStatus.PENDING/SHIPPED/CANCELLED) in domain models. HTTP method enums in frameworks. Role enums in Spring Security. @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING) in JPA — always STRING, never ORDINAL. Strategy pattern where each enum constant overrides an abstract method.

problems.title: Count Word Frequencies Using Map
problems.description:
Count word frequencies from a list of strings using `HashMap<String, Integer>`.

**Example:**
countWords(["apple","banana","apple","cherry","banana","apple"])
=> {apple=3, banana=2, cherry=1}

**Key insight:** `map.merge(word, 1, Integer::sum)` atomically increments count. `getOrDefault` is the alternative.
problems.hint1: Split the string on \s+ and lowercase each word.
problems.hint3: Use a TreeMap so keys are naturally sorted.
problems.testCases:
the cat sat on the mat the cat
{cat=2, mat=1, on=1, sat=1, the=3}

problems.title: Day Type Classifier with Enum
problems.description:
Create a `DayType` enum with values MONDAY–SUNDAY. Implement `classify(DayType day)` returning "Weekend" or "Weekday".

**Example:**
classify(DayType.SATURDAY) => "Weekend"
classify(DayType.MONDAY) => "Weekday"
classify(DayType.SUNDAY) => "Weekend"

**Key insight:** Enums work naturally in `switch` expressions — each case is a compile-time constant.
problems.hint1: Define an enum DayType { WEEKDAY, WEEKEND }.
problems.hint2: Use a switch on the input string (uppercase it first).
problems.hint3: Throw new IllegalArgumentException for default case.

problems.title: Integer Cache Comparison
problems.description:
Demonstrate the Integer autoboxing cache: `Integer.valueOf(n)` caches values in the range [-128, 127].

**Example:**
Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100;
a == b => true (cached)
Integer c = 200; Integer d = 200;
c == d => false (outside cache, new objects)
a.equals(b) => true (always)
c.equals(d) => true (always)

**Key insight:** Never use `==` to compare Integer objects — always use `.equals()` or `intValue()`.
problems.pattern: Integer cache, object identity vs equality
problems.hint1: Java caches Integer instances from -128 to 127.
problems.hint2: Integer.valueOf() uses the cache; new Integer() (deprecated) never does.
problems.testCases:
100 == 100  ref: true
200 == 200  ref: false
50 valueOf ref: true
